content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission SUPERSEDED
by 18702.1(a)(4)

November 10, 1992


Peter E. Tracy
Town Attorney
Town of Mammoth Lakes
P.O. Box 485
Bishop, CA 93514

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-92-593

Dear Mr. Tracy:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Mammoth Lakes Town Councilmember David Watson concerning
his duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act ("Act").¹

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In ore Ogelsby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

Will Councilmember Watson have a conflict of interest with
respect to decisions affecting a person from whom he leases
property?

CONCLUSION

Councilmember Watson will have a conflict of interest with
respect to any decision which will have a foreseeable and material
financial effect on his leasehold interest. Councilmember Watson

________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear in 2 California Code of Regulations section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-92-593
Page 2


will not have a conflict of interest with respect to his landlord
so long as the landlord is not a source of income or gifts of $250
or more to Councilmember Watson.

FACTS

Councilmember Watson is a Mammoth Lakes Town Councilmember.
The councilmember resides in the jurisdiction in a condominium
held by means of a lease from the owners, Acuff Trust.

You stated that the lease agreement is a typical landlord/
tenant agreement and that the councilmember pays the market rate
for the condominium. You stated during our telephone conversation
of November 2, 1992, that the lease was for a fixed period greater
than month-to-month.

The Acuff Trust is a shareholder in the Lodestar Company
which is a current applicant for development approvals
before the town council. The Acuff Trust also owns some property
that is the subject of these decisions. You stated in our
telephone conversation of November 2, 1992, that the property on
which the councilmember's leasehold interest is located is not
subject to these decisions.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 provides:

No public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in making
or in any way attempt to use his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.

Section 87103 provides:

An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
or on:

* * *

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending

Our File No. A-92-593
Page 3


     institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

                         * * *

     (e)  Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

     For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.

     You stated that Councilmember Watson leases property in the
jurisdiction.  Section 82033 provides that a "interest in real
property" includes any leasehold, beneficial or ownership interest
in real property located in the jurisdiction owned directly,
indirectly or beneficially by the public official if the fair
market value of the interest is $1,000 or more.2

     Clearly, Councilmember Watson has a real property interest in
his condominium which is potentially disqualifying.3  (Section
82033; Section 87103(b).)  Thus, he may not participate in any
decision which will have a foreseeable and material financial
effect on his leasehold interest.

     The owner of the property may also be a source of income or
gifts to the councilmember.  The definition of "income" would
include loans and forgiveness of indebtedness.  (Section 82030.)
However, you have not indicated that the councilmember's lease
payments were ever forgiven or are past due.


__________________________
2    A month-to-month tenancy is not a leasehold interest under the
Act.  (Regulation 18233.)

3    Regulation 18729 provides that the value of a leasehold
interest is the amount of rent owed during a 12-month period.

Our File No. A-92-593
Page 4


     Section 82028 defines "gift" to include a rebate or discount
in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is
made in the regular course of business to members of the public
without regard to official status.  However, you stated that the
councilmember held the property by way of a traditional lease and
paid the market rate for the condominium.

     Thus, according to your facts, the owner of the condominium
would not be a "source of income" or "gifts" to the councilmember.

Leasehold Interests in Real Property

     The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that they will occur.  To be
foreseeable, the effects of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however certainty is not required.  (In re Thorner
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

     Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official's economic interest in a decision is
"material" as required by Section 87103.  If the official's
economic interest is directly involved in the decision, then
Regulation 18702.1 applies to determine materiality.  However,
Regulation 18702.1(a)(3) applies to interests in real property
other than leasehold interests.4  (Commission Memorandum [July 2,
1985] Re: Regulation 18702.1.)  Thus, subdivision (a)(3) would not
apply to leasehold interests in real property.

     Please note that Regulation 18702.1(a)(4) provides an
alternate standard of materiality where none of the other
provisions of Regulation 18702.1(a) apply.  Pursuant to Regulation
18702.1(a)(4), if the decision will result in the councilmember's
personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities increasing or
decreasing by $250 or more, then the effect of the decision is
material and the councilmember may not participate.  (Scott Advice
Letter, No. I-89-519.)

     Even if the councilmember's economic interests will not be
directly affected by the decision (or the direct effect is not
material), the councilmember is still required to disqualify
himself where a decision will have an indirect material financial
effect on his economic interests.  Regulation 18702.4 provides
that the indirect financial effect of a decision on a leasehold
interest in real property is material if:

_________________________

4     Prior to a 1988 amendment to Regulation 18702.1, the language
in subdivision (a)(3) originally constituted the entire
subdivision (a)(3) applicable to real property interests.  This
language expressly excluded leasehold interests from its scope.
In 1988, subdivision (a)(3) was redesignated subdivision (a)(3)(A)
and other subdivisions ((B) through (E)) were added.  The language
excluding leasehold interests from the scope of the various new
subdivisions appears to have been inadvertently omitted.

Our File No. A-92-593
Page 5


     (a) The decision will change the legally
allowable use of the leased property, and the
lessee has a right to sublease the property;

     (b) It is reasonably foreseeable that the
lessee will change the actual use of the property
as a result of the decision;

     (c) It is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will result in a change in the actual use
of property within 300 feet of the leased property,
and the changed use will significantly enhance or
significantly decrease the use or enjoyment of the
leased property;

     (d) The decision will increase or decrease
the amount of rent for the leased property by $250
or 5-percent, whichever is greater, during any 12-
month period following the decision; or

     (e) The decision will result in a change in
the termination date of the lease.

                        Regulation 18702.4.

     According to your facts, the decisions in question will not
have these effects on the councilmember's leasehold interests. So
long as the decisions will not affect the councilmember directly
(as stated above) or indirectly in any of the manners listed in
Regulation 18702.4, the councilmember may participate in the
decisions.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to call me at (916) 322-5901.5

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        By: John W. Wallace
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW


5    Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
